title: Dog Grass Seed in Ear: Symptoms, Risks, and Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog shaking their head? A grass seed in the ear is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn the signs and why immediate care is needed.
content_html:
Understanding Grass Seed Impaction

A grass seed, often referred to as a "foxtail" or "awn," is a common but dangerous seasonal hazard for dogs. These seeds are shaped like tiny arrows with microscopic barbs that allow them to move in only one direction: forward. When they enter a dog's ear canal, they do not fall out; instead, they continue to burrow deep toward the sensitive structures of the inner ear.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a grass seed in the ear is High. Because these seeds are migratory, they can quickly reach the eardrum, causing excruciating pain and potentially leading to a rupture or deep-seated infection. If your dog is suddenly shaking their head or pawing at their ear after a walk, you should contact a veterinarian immediately. This condition will not resolve on its own and typically requires professional extraction.

How Photos Help With Triage

While the seed itself may be hidden deep within the canal, providing your veterinarian with a clear photo of the outer ear and the ear canal opening is highly beneficial. A photo allows the medical team to assess the severity of inflammation, redness, or discharge. This visual information helps the clinic prioritize your dog's case and prepare the necessary equipment or sedation required for a safe and painless removal.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of a grass seed in a dog's ear?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include sudden and violent head shaking, tilting the head to one side, scratching at the ear, whining, and sensitivity when the head or ear is touched.

faqs.question: Can I remove the grass seed at home with tweezers?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to remove a grass seed yourself if it is inside the ear canal. Probing the ear can push the seed deeper, rupture the eardrum, or cause the seed to break, leaving barbs behind that cause infection.

faqs.question: Will my dog need sedation to have the seed removed?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Because the ear canal is very sensitive and the seed may be wedged against the eardrum, sedation ensures your dog remains still and pain-free while the vet uses specialized instruments like an otoscope and alligator forceps to retrieve it.
